paragraphs.text: A Chumash sorcerer slowly arranges twenty elongated stones in a circle around a disc-shaped stone. Suddenly he clashes all the stones together and sprinkles water over them. Smoke rises. Now the rains will come.
In the area that will someday bear the name Ventura, a shaman squeezes an oddly shaped stone in his fist. When he opens his fingers, the stone is moist, proof of its rainmaking power.

At Tejón, an Indian opens a deerskin pouch and removes a stone from where it lies nestled among goose down and chia seeds. He takes out the feathers and the seeds, makes a pile of them, then lights them on fire. Having burned his animal offering to the stone, the Indian places fresh down and seeds in the pouch, feeding the stone, caring for it. He speaks to the stone. "Guard the house because you are very powerful; care for the house when the wind makes it shake."
The magic stones are unusual, alien. They are lens-shaped, metallic; they even seem to rust. Some have faint concentric stratifications. They are surprisingly heavy, yet comfortable to hold.

Where did these magic stones come from? Were they shaped by hand or formed by nature? Why did native tribes throughout much of the Southern California coast have them, call them by similar names and use them in similar ways? These questions and more have baffled researchers since the early 1800s, when Father Gerónimo Boscana from Mission San Juan Capistrano first documented the stones' existence.

Through a dramatic sequence of events, researcher Peter C. Howorth has uncovered a major source of the magic stones. By discovering the location, he has brought fresh insight to one of the great archaeological puzzles on the West Coast.
The search for the origin of the stones began at the turn of this century. . . .

In the early 1900s, anthropologist John Peabody Harrington, who had studied the local Chumash Indians and their culture for decades, noticed that their magic stones were like nothing he had seen on the mainland, yet many mainland natives had them. He decided to find out why. He asked an Indian about the origin of the stones. The man replied vaguely that the stones could be found on an island or at the beach. Harrington purchased some of these stones from the Indians. He continued his research, but the mystery remained unsolved.

In 1981, the late Travis Hudson, curator of anthropology at the Museum of Natural History, discovered an obscure reference to the magic stones' origin in one of Harrington's voluminous multilingual manuscripts. The stones supposedly came from distant Santa Barbara Island, nearly 70 miles offshore from the city of Santa Barbara.

Hudson, knowing my longstanding interest in anthropology, mentioned the mystery of the magic stones' origin to me and his discovery of the clue which suggested that they came from Santa Barbara Island. Acting on Hudson's evidence, he and I organized an expedition to try to locate the source of the magic stones on the island. Anthropologist Thomas Blackburn, one of Hudson's collaborators, joined us.

We combed the island carefully but were disappointed. We could find neither the stones nor the geologic structure that would form their matrix. I speculated that the reference that guided us to this island actually referred to the Channel Islands in general rather than to Santa Barbara Island in particular. Before the turn of the century, when Harrington made his provocative notation, mainland residents called the Channel Islands the Santa Barbara Islands. An error in transcription could easily have steered us in the wrong direction. In any event, we would never find the solution to the puzzle on lonely Santa Barbara Island. We would have to look for the stones on the remaining seven islands and the mainland coast.

Over the ensuing years the puzzle continued to tease me. I tucked the information away in the back of my mind and wondered if anyone would ever discover where Indian sorcerers gathered their magic stones.

In late 1987, a search for historic shipwrecks takes me to San Nicolas Island, the most remote of our eight Channel Islands. The United States Navy has managed San Nicolas since 1947, using it as a testing site for military weaponry. Part of the Navy's mission is to preserve the cultural and historic resources on the island; hence my shipwreck search.

The Navy assigns Matt Klope, a civilian employed by the military as a wildlife biologist, to facilitate my research on the island. Klope also serves as an escort; the Navy has declared the island strictly off-limits to the general public, allowing occasional access to special groups such as professional researchers.

We are up before dawn, rattling across the island in a military truck. A broad sandy cove opens in front of us like a catcher's mitt. We begin searching amid the flotsam and jetsam and soon discover bits of wreckage. Nothing large, nothing historic. I urge Klope on; we hope to walk around the island during our stay.

San Nicolas is a wild, hostile place, yet it seems to welcome those who revel in natural surroundings. Like a half-submerged egg, its smooth flanks flow into the sea just as the sea flows around it. Confronted by sharp winds and eerie rock formations, I begin to think about the mysterious tribe of Indians called Nicoleños who lived here long ago.

Who were these resourceful people? Anthropologists believe they were part of the Gabrielino tribe, who lived south of Malibu. Beyond that, researchers know little about them: only a few of their words and virtually nothing of their culture.

The sculptured sandstone, hewn by the surf, forms a haunting seascape. Empty abalone shells glitter on the bluffs overlooking the shore; Nicoleños once harvested abalones along this lonely coast. The nearby intertidal zone still teems with the marine life that provided sustenance for the island's hardy inhabitants.

Farther on, Klope points out a spot where the Nicoleños chipped a groove under a bluff which seeped fresh water. The groove culminates in a ledge where the Indians could have left a tar-lined basket to collect the dripping water.

We continue our search, walking in the shadow of these forgotten people, exploring the tide pools they once visited. The sandstone seems to retain something of their presence; the rock pools evoke a thousand moods. If only the rocks could speak; if only we could set back the clock a few hundred years.
The otherworldliness of the place strikes me. Something seems unusual here. I notice a dark form imbedded in the sandstone. An oddly shaped rock stares up at me.
A magic stone.
I chip it out of the rocks, handling it carefully.

The little stone is lens-shaped and stratified. It glares at me like a bloodshot eye, oozing rusty tears onto the sandstone. It seems alive, powerful, yet I feel no sense of foreboding. Instead, I feel strangely at peace. Perhaps I was meant to find it. Maybe the rocks have whispered their secret to me after all.
Have I discovered the very spot where Nicoleño sorcerers gathered their magic stones? I silently vow to find the answer.
Matt and I continue walking, thoughtful now. We see evidence of the Nicoleños everywhere.

I remember that George Nidever, a sea otter hunter, wrote that all but one of the remaining Nicoleños were brought to the mainland in 1835. At the last moment, just before the ship was to sail, one woman left the group to search the island for her child, whom she never found. A strong wind forced the ship to sail without the woman. The Lone Woman, as she came to be called, was marooned on San Nicolas Island for eighteen years before Nidever finally discovered her and brought her to the mainland. By that time, her fellow tribe members had completely disappeared. The Lone Woman died a few weeks after her arrival on the mainland and was buried behind the Old Mission.

I shiver a little as the sun arcs lower over the sea. We have reached the west end of the island at last, and the weirdly shaped sandstone formations radiate a golden glow. I wonder if this place held any special significance for the Nicoleños. Something about this island affects me profoundly.

Back on the mainland, the stone burns a hole in my pocket. I telephone Jan Timbrook, associate curator of anthropology at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History and long-time associate of Travis Hudson. She wants to see the stone.

Feeling unsure about my find, I explain that much time has passed since I have seen a pedigreed magic stone from the museum's collection and that what I have is probably a worthless rock. I remind Timbrook that I'm no geologist, so we should have a geologist examine the rock.

Timbrook compares the stone I found on San Nicolas with the magic stones in the museum's collection. It's hard to tell them apart. She also unearths a geologist's hypothesis about where the Indians may have found the magic stones. We are astounded; the description is a blueprint for the San Nicolas site.

I confer with Kathy Conti, a staff associate at the museum, and describe my find. She asks if I have read Henshaw, a linguist who studied Native American languages in the latter part of the nineteenth century. She tells me that in 1885 Henshaw wrote a paper on the Indian vocabularies of this region. The paper, relates Conti, contains a tantalizing footnote.

The footnote is a bombshell. After noting the word Ha-la-ca't, the Chumash word for San Nicolas Island, Henshaw added the cryptic phrase "magicians' stones from this island." I feel a strange thrill at reading the note Henshaw penned more than a century ago. Another piece of the puzzle falls into place.

Meanwhile, a geologist who has worked at San Nicolas Island offers us another clue. The geologist explains that when organic matter becomes trapped in sand with just the right chemical composition, the decaying organic matter attracts iron. Gradually, the layers of sand build, and the iron concentration flattens as the sand hardens into stone. This type of sandstone occurs only on San Nicolas Island, on the three largest northern Channel Islands and along a few parts of the mainland. But the iron concretions do not always occur in this type of sandstone. Certainly I can't remember finding any iron outcrops on those three other islands, nor along the mainland coast.

Joining the list of specialists who have become involved in the project is Thomas Blackburn, the anthropologist who accompanied me on the 1981 search for the magic stones on Santa Barbara Island. Blackburn is enthusiastic and encouraging about my find. Willing to visit San Nicolas with us to look at the site, he will give us a professional opinion on our findings.

With the permission of the Navy, we assemble another expedition to San Nicolas Island. The new team consists of myself, Jan Timbrook, Thomas Blackburn, Kathy Conti and Glenn Allen, a professional photographer who will help document our finds. Together we examine the magic stone site and collect new specimens. Considering the evidence we have already unearthed in our studies on the mainland, Blackburn voices his opinion that this spot was very likely a major source for the sorcerers' magic stones.

Reviewing the process that led us to solve the mystery of the magic stones prompts us to assess the implications of our team's find. Even though we now know that the magic stones almost certainly came from San Nicolas Island, we are still no closer to understanding the enigmatic Nicoleños. Were they just another lonely tribe, isolated from other groups by the savage sea? The Nicoleños would have been able to paddle over to the other islands or to the mainland only during exceptionally calm weather. Certainly they had a precious commodity to trade: stones believed to possess supernatural powers and found nowhere else in this region. The difficulty of transporting such stones from this windswept shore would only enhance their value.
Or were the Nicoleños a cult of shamans who performed sacred rituals on that wild, remote island?

We may never know the answers to these questions. Almost all of the evidence has been swept away by the winds of San Nicolas, making each find precious, each clue vital to the research. We can only piece together the fragments of history left to us, search for more evidence and hope, eventually, to unlock another mystery of the past.
The author and Santa Barbara Magazine deeply appreciate the cooperation of the United States Navy and Matt Klope in helping to make this story possible.
Naturalist, writer and photographer Peter C. Howorth specializes in stories relating to the Channel Islands.
